turns.content: I'm trying to push my new crate to crates.io but the build fails because it seems like cargo publish is just running the same command as a local build. Why does this even work this way? Isn't publishing supposed to be different from building locally?

The reason `cargo publish` works by running the normal build process is that crates.io requires a valid, compilable crate before it will accept any upload. The toolchain checks your project against the same constraints as local development: you must have a valid binary or library artifact produced by the compiler. However, the critical difference lies in how `Cargo.toml` metadata is validated and how the registry handles the actual file transmission. When you run `cargo publish`, cargo first performs a dry-run to verify that your crate compiles successfully on all supported platforms listed in your target specification, then it uploads the resulting artifact files to the remote registry. If any step fails, the entire operation aborts rather than partially updating your version.
Okay, so I need to make sure my crate compiles everywhere before publishing? But what if I accidentally break something and the whole release gets rejected?

Exactly. That is why you should always use `--dry-run` before actually pushing your crate. This flag tells cargo to verify the build process and metadata without uploading anything to the registry. If the dry-run succeeds, you can be confident that your changes follow SemVer rules and don't inadvertently break existing users. You should also maintain a changelog file in your repository so that reviewers understand what changed between versions. Once everything is verified with `--dry-run`, running `cargo publish` will upload your crate to crates.io, but remember that if you later decide to retract it, you must use the yank command.

I just deployed a new version of my crate and it's working on the production site, but I'm wondering if there's a better way to handle this. My current approach uses cargo publish with a simple changelog file, but I keep getting errors about missing metadata fields in Cargo.toml when trying to run the dry-run command locally before pushing.

It sounds like you're hitting those common pitfalls around package metadata validation. The key issue here is that cargo publish actually requires specific fields in your Cargo.toml file even for a dry run, and if any of the required identifiers are missing or malformed, it will fail before attempting to upload anything. You should check that your version number follows SemVer strictly and includes all mandatory metadata like authors and license.

I see now about the metadata requirements in Cargo.toml, but I'm still confused why my dry-run keeps failing. Is it because of the actual file format or something else? I want to make sure I don't break production again.

The failure is likely due to how cargo validates your Cargo.toml structure against crates.io requirements before even considering a network upload. If your changelog file isn't properly formatted or if you're missing required fields like the proper version string format, cargo will reject it immediately rather than trying to push. It's better to validate locally first using cargo publish --dry-run so you can catch these metadata issues without risking the live environment.
Okay, I understand the dry-run helps avoid breaking things. But what about after release? If there's a critical bug in the next version, how do we handle that gracefully?

That's exactly where yanking comes into play when you need to retract a published crate from crates.io. When you identify a critical issue requiring immediate retraction, you can use the yank command to mark the version as withdrawn without deleting it entirely from the registry history. This preserves the changelog and metadata while preventing users from downloading that specific version.
I didn't know about yanking until now. So if I use cargo publish --dry-run successfully, then actually run cargo publish with SemVer tagging, and later find a bug... do I just yank it?

Yes, that's the correct workflow once you've validated everything locally. After confirming your Cargo.toml metadata passes all checks via dry-run and SemVer tagging is applied correctly to your version string, you publish normally. If a critical bug appears post-release before users can download it, then yanking becomes your safety net to prevent further distribution of that problematic version.
Wait, so the dry-run isn't just for checking metadata? Does it also validate something else? I want to be absolutely sure before going live.

The dry-run command performs a full validation simulation that includes both your Cargo.toml metadata checks and verifies the target registry connection parameters. It simulates what cargo publish would do without actually uploading anything, so you can confirm that all required fields are present, SemVer is correctly formatted, and the registry endpoint is accessible before committing to the actual release process.
I think I just need to run cargo publish and it will magically appear on crates.io. Since my library already has the right license file, shouldn that be enough?

That's a common first mistake. Just running cargo publish without checking your metadata is risky because crates.io validates everything strictly before indexing. You need to ensure your Cargo.toml has all required fields like authors, description, keywords, and dependencies so the registry can properly index your package.
Okay, I see the metadata section in my config file. But how do I check if it's actually valid before committing to a full release?

You should use cargo --dry-run first to simulate the upload without actually sending anything to the registry. This helps you verify that your Cargo.toml structure is correct and that all dependencies are resolvable before you risk losing access if something goes wrong during the actual publish.
I ran the dry-run command and it said everything looks fine. But I'm worried about versioning, what happens if people use an old API?

That's exactly why SemVer tagging is important for your crate lifecycle. You should maintain a changelog file to track breaking changes and minor features so downstream users can understand when their code will break or improve. This helps you manage version bumps correctly without accidentally introducing regressions.
So if I made a breaking change, I should bump the major version right? And what if I need to take it back down later?

Yes, for breaking changes you bump the major version according to SemVer rules. If you ever realize your release was wrong and needs retraction, you can yank that specific version from crates.io using cargo --yank. This removes it from public index while keeping your source code safe.
Wait, so I don't just commit the release tag and hope for the best? I need to track my changelog entries before publishing?

Exactly. You should maintain a clear changelog with versioned entries before creating your Git tags. This ensures that when you run cargo publish, every release has proper documentation and metadata attached to it.
